title: The Wonderful Wizard of Oz by L. Frank Baum

word1.definition: A large, flat area of grassland with few or no trees.
word1.sentence: Dorothy lived in the middle of the great Kansas prairies.

word2.definition: A powerful, spinning windstorm that can cause great destruction.
word2.sentence: The cyclone lifted the house right up into the air.

word1.definition: Very unusual or remarkable. Far beyond what is normal or expected.
word1.sentence: The cyclone had set the house down in a land of extraordinary beauty.

word2.definition: A woman who practices magic or witchcraft.
word2.sentence: The Witch of the North called Dorothy a noble sorceress.

word1.definition: Boring and annoying because it lasts too long or happens too often.
word1.sentence: The Scarecrow said it was very tiresome being stuck on a pole all day.

word2.definition: A person or animal you spend time with; a friend who goes with you on a journey.
word2.sentence: Dorothy was glad to have a companion on the road to the Emerald City.

word1.definition: Dark, dim, and depressing. A place or mood that feels heavy and sad.
word1.sentence: The forest was gloomy — the trees blocked out the sunlight.

word2.definition: The thick bushes, plants, and small trees that grow beneath the tall trees in a forest.
word2.sentence: They pushed through the undergrowth and found a man made entirely of tin.

word1.definition: Put under a magical spell. Something that has been changed or affected by magic.
word1.sentence: The Wicked Witch enchanted the Tin Woodman's axe so that it would slip and cut him.

word2.definition: In a gentle, caring, loving way.
word2.sentence: The Tin Woodman said he once loved a girl tenderly, but without a heart, he could no longer feel love.

word1.definition: The ability to do something even when you are afraid. Being brave when it is hard.
word1.sentence: The Lion said he had no courage, yet he leaped out of the trees to frighten the travelers.

word2.definition: When two things are opposite or do not match.
word2.sentence: The Lion is a contradiction: he calls himself a coward, but his actions show bravery.

questions.question: What is Dorothy's life like in Kansas before the cyclone?
options: Gray, flat, and quiet — Uncle Henry and Aunt Em rarely smile or laugh
She lives in a big city with many friends
She goes to school and plays sports

questions.question: The author describes Kansas as gray over and over. Why do you think he does this? What might he be setting up for later in the story?
What happened to the Wicked Witch of the East?
options: She flew away on a broomstick
Dorothy's house landed on her and destroyed her
The Witch of the North defeated her in a battle

questions.question: Dorothy wants to go home to gray, dull Kansas instead of staying in this beautiful, colorful land. Why? What does this tell you about what 'home' means to her?
